title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) genes and GBJ002 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4934, 1:12]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
COG
Regarding COG categories, 9 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 8 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GBJ002_C133, which is enriched in genes related to cell motility (N), and GBJ002_C029c, which includes 23 genes involved in translation, ribosomal structure and biogenesis (J).


Code
cog_results <- cog_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(cog_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("COG", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p value", "Cluster"),
  extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 0, rightColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)

title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
KEGG
Regarding KEGG terms, there is a wide variety across the clusters. GBJ002_C133 is again related to cell motility pathways, while GBJ002_C020a is related to quorum sensing. Regarding replication and repair pathways, we can observe two distinct clusters: GBJ002_C235, associated to DNA replication, homologous recombination and mismatch repair, and GBJ002_C046, associated to nucleotide excision repair. Interestingly, the second biggest cluster (GBJ002_C281) appears to be enriched in beta-lactam resistance genes and different metabolic pathways, while the biggest one (GBJ002_C384) is enriched in 10 different metabolic pathways.


Code
kegg_results <- kegg_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(kegg_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Category", "Subcategory", "Pathway ID", "Pathway description", "Gene ratio",
  "Background ratio", "Rich factor", "Fold enrichment", "Z score", "p value", "Adjusted p value",
  "q value", "Genes", "Gene count", "Cluster"),
  extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 0, rightColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3)

title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
GO
GO enrichment analysis is much more complex, since the presence of few genes can result in different enrichment levels. In any case, three independent tables (BP, MF and CC) have been generated and are summarized in the Enrichment summary section.

title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
EC
Although there are not many EC terms, some interesting results can be observed. For example, cluster GBJ002_C384 is enriched at each of numbers 7, 7.1 and 7.1.2 and by 7 genes, which correspond to subunits of F0F1 ATP synthase.

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) and GIL01 genes and GIL01 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4967, 13:24]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
COG
Regarding COG categories, 9 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 7 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GIL01_C032b, which is enriched in genes related to cell motility (N), and GIL01_C029a, enriched in mobilome: prophages, transposons (X) category.


Code
cog_results <- cog_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(cog_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("COG", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),
  extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 0, rightColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
KEGG
Regarding KEGG terms, there is a wide variety across the clusters. GIL01_C032b is again related to cell motility pathways, while GIL01_C029b, GIL01_C020c and GIL01_C020d are realted to membrane transport pathways. Regarding replication and repair pathways, we can observe two distinct clusters: GIL01_C290, associated to DNA replication, homologous recombination and mismatch repair, and GIL01_C036c, associated to nucleotide excision repair. Interestingly, 9 clusters appears to be enriched in diverse metabolic pathways, which suggests a specialization in gene expression in the different reflected pathways.

kegg_results <- kegg_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) and GIL16 genes and GIL16 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[c(1:4934, 4968:5000), 25:36]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
COG
Regarding COG categories, 11 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 9 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GIL16_C071, which is enriched in genes related to cell cycle control, cell division, chromosome partitioning (D), and GIL16_C019, which includes 5 genes involved in amino acid transport and metabolism (E).


Code
cog_results <- cog_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(cog_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("COG", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),
  extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 0, rightColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
KEGG
Regarding KEGG terms, there is a wide variety across the clusters. GIL16_C036b is related to quorum sensing pathways, while GIL16_C027a is related to membrane transport. Regarding replication and repair pathways, we can observe two distinct clusters: GIL16_C289, associated to DNA replication, mismatch repair, homologous recombination and nucleotide excision repair, and GIL16_C050, associated to the last pathway. Interestingly, 17 clusters appears to be enriched in diverse metabolic pathways, which suggests a specialization in gene expression in the different reflected pathways.

kegg_results <- kegg_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(kegg_results, rownames = FALSE,

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
EC
Although there are not many EC terms, some interesting results can be observed. For example, GIL16_C050 is enriched at each of numbers 7, 7.1 and 7.1.2, which correspond to some F0F1 ATP synthase subunits. Interestingly, other subunits of this enzyme are found in GIL16_C100, as shown in the cluster composition table. In fact, there are other genes not annotated by EC, since this cluster contains up to 6 subunits genes of this enzyme. Another enriched cluster at all three levels is GIL16_C037, specifically at numbers 6, 6.1 and 6.1.1 by the presence of tRNA ligases genes.


Code
ec_1_results <- ec_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes, 1)
datatable(ec_1_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("EC number", "Class", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio",
  "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),

    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"))) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)






Code
ec_2_results <- ec_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes, 2)
datatable(ec_2_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio",
  "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),
  caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
    style = "caption-side: top; text-align: start;",
    "EC level 2 enrichment"),
  extensions = "Buttons", options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"))) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)

ec_3_results <- ec_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes, 3)
datatable(ec_3_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("EC number", "Class", "Subclass", "Sub-subclass", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio",
  "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),
  caption = htmltools::tags$caption(
    style = "caption-side: top; text-align: start;",
    "EC level 3 enrichment"),
  extensions = "Buttons", options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"))) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "odds.ratio", digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("p.value", "padj"), digits = 3)
